revision.text:
[[en:Hokkaidō Gokoku Shrine]][[ja:北海道護國神社]] {{Multiple issues|{{more citations needed|date=June 2023}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 28, 2017}}}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | consecration_year = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | name = Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Hokkaido Gokoku.JPG|250px]]
Haiden | location = {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|qid=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Asahikawa|en|Asahikawa|qid=Q200740|lt=Asahikawa|1=Asahikawa}}, Hanazono-cho 1-2282-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|43|47|17.4|N|142|22|0.3|E|region:JP-01|display=it}} | deity = Those who perished in national service from Hokkaido and Karafuto | consecration_year = Meiji 35 (1902) | Rank = Ministry of Home Affairs-designated Gokoku Shrine, Betto Shrine | festival = | Main hall = | Map = {{Switcher |{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=180|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|coord={{coord|43|47|17.4|N|142|22|0.3|E}}|frame-latitude=43.781|frame-longitude=142.366}} |Show Wikimedia maps |{{location map|Japan Hokkaido|lat_deg=43|lat_min=47|lat_sec=17.4|lon_deg=142|lon_min=22|lon_sec=0.3|label=Hokkaido
Gokoku Shrine|position=top|width=210|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|AlternativeMap=Hokkaidō géolocalisation 000.svg}} |Show Hokkaido map }} }} '''Hokkaidō Gokoku Shrine''' (北海道護国神社, ''Hokkaidō Gokoku jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Asahikawa, Hokkaido|Asahikawa, Hokkaido]] at 1 Chome-2282-2 Hanasakicho, Asahikawa, Hokkaido 070-0901. It was established in 1902, and enshrines [[Raijin]] (雷電大神), [[Sarutahiko Ōkami]] (猿田彦大神), and other [[kami]]. It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead. Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|qid=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|qid=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} 63,141 people are enshrined there.{{Cite web |title=Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine - Asahikawa - TracesOfWar.com |url=https://www.tracesofwar.com/sights/136628/Hokkaido-Gokoku-Shrine.htm |access-date=2023-06-09 |website=www.tracesofwar.com |language=en}} '''Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine''' (北海道護國神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} located in {{ill|Asahikawa|en|Asahikawa|qid=Q200740|lt=Asahikawa|1=Asahikawa}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|qid=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}. It enshrines those who died in service to the nation, related to Hokkaido and Karafuto. Initially established as the Hokkaido Shōkonsha in 1939, it was renamed Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine. The shrine honors 63,141 spirits of those who perished from the Boshin War to the Greater East Asia War (Pacific War). == History == The shrine traces its origin to May 5-6, Meiji 35 (1902), when the first Shōkon Festival was conducted by {{ill|Ōsako Naoharu|en|Ōsako Naoharu|qid=Q1150431|lt=General Viscount Oosako Naotoshi|1=Ōsako Naoharu}} of the {{ill|7th_Division_(Imperial_Japanese_Army)|en|7th_Division_(Imperial_Japanese_Army)|qid=Q4644015|lt=7th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|1=7th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)}}. Subsequently, the shrine designated this as the year of its founding. In Meiji 43 (1910), Lieutenant General {{ill|Uehara_Yūsaku|en|Uehara_Yūsaku|qid=Q128281|lt=Uehara Yūsaku|1=Uehara Yūsaku}}, in conjunction with the governor of {{ill|Hokkaido Prefecture (1886-1947)|ja|北海道庁 (1886-1947)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Prefecture (1886-1947)|qq=}}, submitted a petition for the shrine's construction. Construction was completed in May 1912, with the dedication ceremony held on June 4, followed by the grand festival on June 5-6. In Taishō 5 (1916), the shrine was renamed Hokkaido Shōkonjō. On June 1, Shōwa 9 (1934), a petition for the establishment of the Hokkaido Shōkonsha was submitted, and the shrine was a11roved by the Ministry of the Interior on April 26, Shōwa 10 (1935). Renamed and designated as a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|ja|指定護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 護国神社}} on April 1, Shōwa 14 (1939), it became Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine. In 1944, with the creation of Karafuto Gokoku Shrine, the scope of enshrined spirits was adjusted. The shrine underwent significant changes during the postwar period under the {{ill|Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|qid=Q3029647|lt=Shinto Directive|1=Shinto Directive}} issued by the Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers. On October 11, Shōwa 21 (1946), it was renamed simply "Hokkaido Shrine" to evade scrutiny. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1951, the original name, Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine, was restored. Notable visits include those of {{ill|Nobuhito,_Prince_Takamatsu|en|Nobuhito,_Prince_Takamatsu|qid=Q1030368|lt=Prince Takamatsu Nobuhito|1=Nobuhito, Prince Takamatsu}} in 1954, {{ill|Takahito,_Prince_Mikasa|en|Takahito,_Prince_Mikasa|qid=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa Takahito|1=Takahito, Prince Mikasa}} in the same year, and Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun in 1968. The shrine became an independent religious corporation in 1958. == Notes == {{Reflist}} == See also == * {{ill|Hokkaido Grand Parade of Marching Bands|ja|北海道音楽大行進|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Grand Parade of Marching Bands|qq=}} *[[List of Shinto shrines in Japan#Hokkaid.C5.8D|List of Shinto shrines in Hokkaidō]] * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|qid=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|qid=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|qid=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|qid=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|qid=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|qid=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|qid=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|qid=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|qid=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|qid=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|qid=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|qid=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} == References == {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.hokkaido-gokoku.org Official website] *[https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北海道護国神社/ Hokkaido Shinto listing] *[http://hokkaido-gokoku.org/ Official website of Hokkaido Gokoku Shrine] {{Authority control}} {{coord|43.78814|142.36675|format=dms|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title}} {{Gokoku shrines}}{{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|北海道護國神社|version=99513693|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:北海道護國神社]] [[en:Hokkaidō Gokoku Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11403068}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Asahikawa]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:History of Asahikawa]]
